content.full_text:
J. P. CORREIA
LARRY C. BACON
ROBERT F. KASTER II

CORREIA, BACON & KASTER
ATTORNEYS AT LAW
701 FOURTH STREET
YREKA, CALIF. 96097

June 14, 1976

AREA CODE 916
TELEPHONE 842-4193
POST OFFICE BOX 510

Delbert Spurlock
Fair Political Practices
Commission
1100 K Street
Sacramento, California 95814

Dear Mr. Spurlock:

In respect of the proposed action of the City of Weed
against Mobil Oil Corporation, this will restate the problem
presented.

I own stock of Mobil Oil Corporation of a value of in excess
of $1,000 and am City Attorney of the City of Weed. The
City of Weed proposes to sue Mobil Oil Corporation to abate
a nuisance, namely a tall oil company sign with plastic
faces which have become deteriorated as a result of buffeting by the high winds and cold temperatures. As a result of
the deterioration, large segments of plastic materials fall
and may cause personal injury or death and may cause property damage. The question presented is whether or not the
ownership of the stock in question is sufficient to preclude
me from proceeding with the litigation on behalf of the
City.

It is requested that you render an opinion on the subject.

Very truly yours,

[signature]
CORREIA,
City Attorney
City of Weed

JPC:cw

Delbert Spurlock                    -2-                    June 14, 1976


     PS: The stock owned by me does not exceed $13,000
in value and the annual income therefrom does not exceed
$1,000.

                                        JPC

sections.question: The question presented is whether or not the ownership of the stock in question is sufficient to preclude me from proceeding with the litigation on behalf of the City.
sections.question_synthetic: Whether a City Attorney's ownership of Mobil Oil Corporation stock valued between $1,000 and $13,000 creates a conflict of interest that would preclude the attorney from representing the city in litigation against Mobil Oil Corporation.
sections.parsing_notes: No section headers found; LLM: This is an incoming request letter to FPPC, not an FPPC response. No FPPC conclusion or advice is provided in this document. The letter is from an attorney (Correia) requesting an FPPC opinion. Document contains clear question section but no conclusion/short answer section.

embedding.qa_text: QUESTION: The question presented is whether or not the ownership of the stock in question is sufficient to preclude me from proceeding with the litigation on behalf of the City.
embedding.first_500_words: This is a letter from CORREIA, BACON & KASTER, Attorneys at Law, to Delbert Spurlock of the Fair Political Practices Commission in Sacramento, California. The letter discusses the proposed action of the City of Weed against Mobil Oil Corporation and presents a legal question regarding ownership of stock. The letter states: Delbert Spurlock Fair Political Practices Commission 1100 K Street Sacramento, California 95814 Dear Mr. Spurlock: In respect of the proposed action of the City of Weed against Mobil Oil Corporation, this will restate the problem presented. I own stock of Mobil Oil Corporation of a value of in excess of $1,000 and am City Attorney of the City of Weed. The City of Weed proposes to sue Mobil Oil Corporation to abate a nuisance, namely a tall oil company sign with plastic faces which have become deteriorated as a result of buffeting by the high winds and cold temperatures. As a result of the deterioration, large segments of plastic materials fall and may cause personal injury or death and may cause property damage. The question presented is whether or not the ownership of the stock in question is sufficient to preclude me from proceeding with the litigation on behalf of the City. It is requested that you render an opinion on the subject. Very truly yours, [signature] CORREIA, City Attorney City of Weed Delbert Spurlock June 14, 1976 PS: The stock owned by me does not exceed $13,000 in value and the annual income therefrom does not exceed $1,000. JPC
embedding.summary: City Attorney Correia requests FPPC advice on whether his personal ownership of Mobil Oil Corporation stock (valued between $1,000-$13,000 with annual income under $1,000) creates a conflict of interest that would prevent him from representing the City of Weed in litigation against Mobil Oil to abate a deteriorated company sign.
